one_line_takeaway: A 2025 survey that organizes the DRL adversarial-security literature into a component-based attack taxonomy (state/reward/action/model) and a five-family defense taxonomy, and argues the field's core gap is attack-specific defenses plus the absence of any standardized robustness benchmark.
key_finding: Consolidation-level findings rather than a measured result: (1) most adversarial attacks on DRL target the state/observation space; reward-, action-, and model-space attacks are comparatively under-explored. (2) Every defense family is attack-specific -- none defends comprehensively against unknown threats -- and the field has no unified evaluation standard, so cross-method comparison is unreliable. (3) RL's temporal dependency makes attack design and evaluation harder than in supervised learning because effects must propagate over multiple steps. (4) Adversarial training dominates defenses but is computationally expensive and generalizes poorly; defensive distillation alone is insufficient (citing Carlini et al.) and must be combined with other mechanisms.
core_contribution: A survey (not an empirical paper) of adversarial attacks and defenses against deep reinforcement learning (DRL), covering representative work published 2021-2025. Its own contributions are organizational rather than experimental: (1) an attack taxonomy that classifies adversarial attacks on DRL by which functional component of the DRL loop is perturbed -- state space (observations + environment), reward function, action space, and model space; and (2) a defense taxonomy organized by intervention level into five categories -- adversarial training, competitive training, robust learning, adversarial detection, and defensive distillation. The authors position this as the first systematic review focused specifically on DRL (as opposed to prior surveys centered on supervised classification), and add a background section formalizing RL/POMDP, robust RL (min-max objective), and DNN vulnerabilities, plus a discussion of six open challenges.
threat_model: The survey frames DRL adversaries around the POMDP components an attacker can reach. Attack surfaces catalogued: observation channel (perturb agent's perceptual inputs to induce misperception; assumes attacker can access or infer observations), environment dynamics (poison state-transition rules / reward function / embed triggers), reward function (poison reward signals or preference feedback, incl. offline datasets and RLHF), action space (directly perturb action outputs or the selection mechanism), and model space (alter weights/architecture, or extract/clone the policy via black-box queries). Attacker knowledge spans white-box, gray-box, and black-box; timing spans train-time (poisoning) and test-time (evasion). Recurring emphasis on two constraints unique to RL: stealthiness (perturbations must not create obvious anomalies) and effectiveness across the temporal/multi-step horizon (attack effects must propagate to reduce long-term cumulative return, not just single-step decisions).
limitations: As a survey it presents no original experiments, so all quantitative claims are inherited from cited works and are not independently verified or re-run under a common protocol. The paper itself flags that the surveyed field lacks standardized benchmarks, making the tabulated comparisons descriptive rather than apples-to-apples. Coverage is largely descriptive/qualitative with little quantitative meta-analysis of relative attack/defense strength. The PDF has some rendering artifacts (e.g. a broken 'Figure??' reference, an inconsistent author-name spelling 'Becror/Bector'). The reference list is heavily weighted toward 2021-2025 arXiv preprints. Naming of one taxonomy element ('Data-centric Robust Learning', abbreviated DRL in Yang et al.) collides confusingly with the survey's own use of DRL.

datasets_used: No datasets of the authors' own -- survey only. Environments/benchmarks recurring across the SURVEYED works (per Tables 1-2): Atari (Pong, Breakout, Freeway, Qbert, Space Invaders, Boxing, Enduro, Assault)
MuJoCo continuous-control (Hopper, Walker2d, HalfCheetah, Humanoid, InvertedPendulum, Ant, Swimmer)
OpenAI Gym / classic control (CartPole, MountainCar, Pendulum, LunarLander, BipedalWalker)
D4RL offline-RL (HalfCheetah, Hopper) and Meta-World / highway-env robotic and driving tasks

Multi-agent benchmarks: SMAC / StarCraft II, Predator-Prey, MPE, MAMuJoCo; plus grid-worlds, SUMO traffic, Containernet, tactical MANETs, and image datasets (CIFAR-10/100, TinyImageNet) used by transfer-attack robustness work
quantitative_results: State/observation perturbations can cause accuracy drops of up to 40-60% in well-trained DRL agents in highly uncertain open environments (attributed to ref [34], not measured by the survey)

Sarkar et al.'s Asynchronous Reward Injection (ARI): targeted variant induces the intended target action in 75% of target states, and the untargeted variant degrades DQN Atari performance to near-random (reported from cited work)

Table 1 tabulates 24 surveyed attack papers across DRL algorithm, environment, white/black-box, train/test-time, and objective (state/action/reward/model); Table 2 tabulates ~20 surveyed defense papers across five defense families -- these are counts/coverage from the survey's own tables, not performance numbers
No experiment, ablation, or benchmark result is produced by the paper itself; every numeric claim is secondhand from a cited study
baselines_compared: No baselines run by the authors. The survey instead qualitatively contrasts the five defense families against each other: adversarial training (robust but costly, poor generalization) vs competitive/game-theoretic training (better resilience, convergence issues in high dimensions) vs robust learning (distributional robustness / parameter noise) vs adversarial detection (extra guarantees, weak on stealthy attacks) vs defensive distillation (reduces small-perturbation sensitivity, weak vs high-intensity/non-gradient attacks)

Surveyed methods are described relative to the baselines used in their own original papers (e.g. SAIA vs random-perturbation baselines; SRIM vs one-shot/random attacks; RankPoison, TEPA, CEP, PCA vs prior poisoning baselines) -- reported, not reproduced
